name: Boyer-Moore
def: Substring search that scans the pattern right-to-left and skips ahead using bad-character and good-suffix rules.
problem: Search large-alphabet text faster than linear by skipping regions that cannot match — often sublinear in practice.
mechanism: Align the pattern and compare from its right end; on a mismatch, shift by the larger of the bad-character and good-suffix heuristics, frequently jumping past many positions at once.
benefits: Sublinear average time on large alphabets
Larger skips as the pattern grows
limits: Naive worst case O(nm) — Boyer-Moore-Galil fixes it
failures: Off-by-one bugs in the good-suffix table
Assuming linearity without the Galil rule
whenNot: Streaming text that cannot backtrack (use KMP)
myths: "KMP is the fastest exact matcher" — Boyer-Moore variants usually win in practice for a single pattern
